The main claim is: However, if successful I think this seems like it could be very competitive with other approaches to alignment research.
I think the key here is: can we train a system X which can safely help us to pursue alignment research, before we have a mechanistic understanding of how system X is working?
The main claim is: We believe that even without fundamentally new alignment ideas, we can likely build sufficiently aligned AI systems to substantially advance alignment research itself.
It might not be fundamentally easier to align models that can meaningfully accelerate alignment research than it is to align AGI.

The Gap ------- There’s still a wide gap to cross before we can directly use Selection Theorems for interpretability.

The main claim is: 51-54): “certain objects are such that their description is more complex than the object itself.” We can explicate this proposition on a theoretical level in the domain of optical patterns.
A simple object requires a short (quasi-linguistic) description but yields a diffuse recognition spot; hence it is identified most readily by quasi-linguistic representation or description.
